finding: Prediction claims 'global' decay but confirmation uses SAA-only data (~30 nT/yr at Tsumeb); 28 nT/yr is a model input parameter, not a derived output; threshold set within established trend guarantees confirmation
detail_claim: The global geomagnetic field decayed by at least 28 nT in 12 months (PROS-007).
detail_evidence: PROS-007 claims the "global geomagnetic field would have decayed by at least 28 nT" in 12 months — but the dome's own confirmation text admits the measurement is regional: "SAA region alone dropped ~30 nT since January 2025." The SAA core experiences 20–35 nT/yr decay (NCEI/WMM), but this is a regional feature driven by reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary, not representative of the global dipole. The global dipole moment has declined ~5% per century since 1840 (Thébault et al., 2015, IGRF-12). Translating this to surface intensity: 5% of ~30,000 nT equatorial field over 100 years gives ~15 nT/yr as a global average — well below the 28 nT/yr threshold, which is only met at SAA-core stations like Tsumeb. The dome's own confirmation text reveals the bait-and-switch: it predicts 'global geomagnetic field would have decayed by at least 28 nT' but confirms with 'SAA region alone dropped ~30 nT since January 2025' — the word 'alone' tacitly admitting the global field may not meet the threshold. Moreover, the confirmation uses a ~14-month window (January 2025–March 2026) rather than the 12 months specified in the prediction; annualized, ~30 nT over 14 months = 25.7 nT/yr — below the dome's own 28 nT/yr threshold. WIN-037's confirmation is a static W011 entry (~30 nT over the dome's 14-month reporting window, no live recomputation). The dome's live Domain 8 panel ("SAA Decay Rate") reinforces the same geographic limitation: it reports Predicted 77.0 nT/yr = Observed 77.0 nT/yr against INTERMAGNET Tsumeb only — confirming the dome's monitoring looks nowhere outside the SAA core. Furthermore, the dome lists "Field decay rate: ≥28 nT/year" as a core model parameter — an input adopted from observations, not an output derived from dome geometry. Measuring the value you built into your model is circular, not confirmatory. PROS-007 was registered 6 March 2026 and confirmed by 12 March 2026 — a 6-day turnaround using WMM2025 data publicly available since December 2024. This pattern (register prediction → confirm with already-public data within days) recurs across the batch of five predictions (WIN-035 through WIN-039), all registered on 2026-03-06, suggesting systematic data mining from already-published sources rather than prospective prediction. WIN-042 makes the mathematically equivalent claim framed as an annualized rate (≥28 nT/yr) rather than a 12-month cumulative threshold. Because 28 nT/year (rate) and 28 nT over 12 months (cumulative) are numerically identical under a constant-rate assumption, WIN-037 and WIN-042 collapse into a single claim with two labels — the same measurement expressed twice does not constitute two independent confirmations.
detail_verdict_text: 28 nT/yr applies to SAA region only; the global dipole moment declines at ~5% per century (Thébault et al., 2015). Expressing this as surface field intensity: ~5% of ~30,000 nT equatorial field over 100 years gives ~15 nT/yr as a global average — well below the 28 nT/yr threshold, which is only met at SAA-core stations like Tsumeb. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim raises post-hoc concerns despite having a genuinely prospective test window. The dome claims prospective status via OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring, and for this WIN, the dome's own source dates PROS-007 registration as 6 March 2026 — the final week of the claimed March 2025–March 2026 measurement window. By registration, ~99% of the 12-month decay had already occurred; the observation was essentially complete. This is retrodiction of a known trend timestamped at the window's close, not prospective prediction. OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file (see Timestamp Error), and the predicted threshold of ≥28 nT matches the SAA's established decay rate (~28–30 nT/yr at Tsumeb, per NCEI/WMM data) — it is the model's own input parameter being confirmed by the same ongoing trend it was fitted to. Even granting the timing, this is a non-discriminating extrapolation: both dome and WMM2025 predict the same continued decay from the same station data.
detail_extra: See also WIN-042, which applies the same ≥28 nT/yr threshold to a different time window — potentially counting the same ongoing observation twice. Timestamp note: This WIN is claimed as prospective via OpenTimestamps — but OTS anchors the observation file, not the prediction. See Timestamp Error.

code_analysis.monitoring_evidence: The W011 confirmation (~30 nT dropped) is static text in raw-text/02-wins.txt. Domain 8 live panel shows 77.0 nT/yr PRED=OBS identity (different number, different logic). No live recomputation path for the 28 nT threshold.

tldr_evidence: The dome claims the 'global' field decayed by ≥28 nT in 12 months, but its own confirmation text admits the measurement is regional — the SAA alone dropped ~30 nT. The global dipole moment declines much more slowly (~15 nT/year averaged over the whole surface). The dome inflates a regional number into a global claim.
tldr_verdict: 28 nT/year is a regional SAA number, not global. The global decline is roughly half that. Expressing a local measurement as a global prediction is misleading.

finding: Dome's own cavity minimum is 8.78 Hz at the pole (its most favorable point); 7.83 Hz is below the geometric floor at every radius; stability is textbook ionospheric physics
detail_claim: Schumann resonance remains stable at 7.83 Hz, proving dome cavity.
detail_evidence:
The pole-floor impossibility (lead argument). The dome's own cavity height formula H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km has a strict global maximum of 8,537 km at r = 0 and decreases monotonically outward. Applying the dome's own quarter-wave formula f = c/(4H) — the same formula WIN-029 uses to back-derive a 9,572 km ceiling from 7.83 Hz — to this height profile gives the dome's minimum possible resonance frequency at the pole: f(r = 0) = 299,792 / (4 × 8,537) ≈ 8.78 Hz. Because H(r) falls monotonically, f(r) rises monotonically: at r = 5,960 km (the dome's published Chapel Hill latitude) H ≈ 4,278 km and f ≈ 17.5 Hz; at r = 10,000 km, f ≈ 27 Hz; at the 20,015 km disc rim, f ≈ 90 Hz. 7.83 Hz is below the dome's own geometric floor at every point on the disc. No weighting scheme — area-weighted, radial, energy-weighted, or infinitely pole-concentrated — can drive an average below 8.78 Hz when 8.78 Hz is the minimum of the function being averaged. This is not a calibration question; it is a mathematical impossibility inherited directly from the dome's published H(r).

Averaging backup (reinforcement, not the main argument). Even setting the pole-floor aside, no physically motivated spatial average over the disc lands anywhere near 7.83 Hz either. Effective heights ranging from ~1,800 km (area-weighted) to ~5,200 km (energy-weighted) yield predicted fundamentals of 14–42 Hz, a spread that brackets the simple radial average (~3,400 km → ~22 Hz). Every averaging choice is wrong, and all of them are wrong in the same direction: higher than observed. The averaging framing is a softer way to state what the pole-floor already settles absolutely.

Dome's own triple-count. WIN-002 (damping explains 10.59 → 7.83), WIN-029 (7.83 back-derives H = 9,572 km), and WIN-038 (7.83 remains stable) all derive from a single Tomsk Schumann measurement. WIN-029's back-derived ceiling of 9,572 km exceeds the dome's own V12 maximum of 8,537 km at r = 0 by ~12% — a second-order internal contradiction (the dome's observed-frequency-to-cavity-height inversion demands a cavity taller than its own formula permits). See Section 2.1 for the full pole-floor and supremum-impossibility treatment, and WIN-029 / WIN-002 for the duplicate-counting cross-reference.

The "quarter-wave doesn’t apply at planetary scale" escape is closed in advance (preemptive). The strongest available defense against the pole-floor argument is to say f = c/(4H) is a parallel-plate simplification that breaks down at planetary scale, and the real Schumann mechanism is a transverse (TM) mode running around the cavity’s horizontal dimension: fn = (c / 2πR) × √(n(n+1)) ≈ 10.59 Hz for n = 1 on a 6,371 km sphere (Schumann 1952; formalized in Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002), with the observed 7.83 Hz produced by ionospheric damping. This is correct globe physics — and it cannot rescue the dome for four compounding reasons. (i) The dome is already on record using quarter-wave, and WIN-029’s numerical match vanishes if the dome retreats. The model page (V50.6, “Schumann Cavity Height”) writes H = c / (4 × f_Schumann) = 299,792 / (4 × 7.83) = 9,572 km, and WIN-029 is the credit claim for that exact inversion. A retreat to transverse modes does not save WIN-038 in isolation; it requires the author to simultaneously withdraw WIN-029 and delete the 9,572 km firmament-height figure propagated throughout the model. And the retreat is not free: WIN-029’s “confirmed” status rests specifically on the 5.3% agreement between the 9,572 km back-derivation and the model’s 9,086 km parameterization. That quantitative match is the entire reason WIN-029 is scored as a confirmation rather than a tautological unit conversion — and a retreat from quarter-wave erases the 5.3% agreement entirely, because the transverse-mode formula has no cavity-height output at all. WIN-029 stripped of the 9,572 km number collapses from “confirmation of a 9,086 km firmament” to the qualitative restatement “a conductive cavity exists,” which every globe textbook already grants. You cannot have quarter-wave for the back-derivation you want and transverse-mode for the forward-derivation you need. (ii) Transverse mode imports a globe fingerprint. The spherical transverse formula reproduces 7.83 Hz only when the wavelength-setting length equals Earth’s circumference, 2πREarth = 40,030 km using the mean-radius R = 6,371 km (the WGS84 equatorial circumference is 40,075 km and is the figure quoted in STEELMAN-03; the AE-projection identity disc_radius = π × R holds under either choice). The dome’s published disc diameter is 2 × 20,015 = 40,030 km — identical to four significant figures, because disc_radius = π × REarth is exactly the azimuthal-equidistant projection identity. Plugging dome disc diameter into the globe’s transverse formula is the classic tautology flagged in Part 6 (STEELMAN-03: Tesla Disc Diameter Lock): a number built from globe geometry by construction cannot independently confirm dome geometry. (iii) The dome’s non-uniform H(r) violates the transverse-mode boundary conditions. The fn ≈ 10.59 Hz formula assumes a closed, approximately uniform spherical shell; the dome’s H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r / 8,619) km varies by roughly a factor of ten across the disc (8,537 km at the pole down to ≈836 km at the 20,015 km rim) and terminates at an open rim. Borrowing the sphere’s mode formula without the sphere’s geometry is precisely the silent formula-switch already documented in Section 2.1. (iv) The natural disc-cavity escape — cylindrical-waveguide Bessel modes — also fails. A sophisticated defender may object that the honest transverse analog for a disc-with-ceiling is neither vertical quarter-wave nor spherical TM but a cylindrical-waveguide mode governed by Bessel-function zeros: f = jn,m · c / (2πa). For the dome’s disc of radius a = 20,015 km, the leading cutoffs are TM01 at j0,1 · c / (2πa) = 2.4048 · 299,792 / (2π · 20,015) ≈ 5.73 Hz, TM11 at ≈ 9.13 Hz, and TE11 at ≈ 4.39 Hz (Bessel zeros: DLMF §10.21; cylindrical-waveguide formula: Collin, Field Theory of Guided Waves, 2nd ed., Ch. 5). None match 7.83 Hz from dome geometry alone, and the one closest to it (TM11 at 9.13 Hz) requires the same a = 20,015 km that is π · REarth by the AE-projection identity — the globe fingerprint is imported a second time. (A defender may reply that the honest calculation is a coupled radial-vertical eigenvalue problem on the dome’s specific H(r), not the flat cylinder. Granted — but that problem still takes a = 20,015 km = π · REarth as its radial boundary, so the globe-fingerprint is imported regardless of the detailed spectrum; and Bessel-zero monotonicity plus domain containment (the dome cavity is strictly contained in the flat cylinder with H = 8,537 km) put the dome’s fundamental TM-like mode at ≥5.73 Hz as a lower bound. The dome has not computed or published any coupled-eigenvalue result that lands at 7.83 Hz.) There is no mode formula the dome has published, nor any it could borrow from globe physics or cylindrical-waveguide theory, that reaches 7.83 Hz from dome geometry alone: either the pole-floor holds (quarter-wave), or the globe is assumed (spherical transverse), or the disc-cavity Bessel modes miss by 20–45% while still requiring globe-circumference geometry to even be written down.

Stability is ionospheric physics, not dome evidence. The long-term stability of the Schumann fundamental near 7.83 Hz is well-established in standard atmospheric physics (Sentman 1987; Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002, ISBN 978-1-4020-0754-5). The frequency varies diurnally by ~0.5 Hz and shifts by up to ~1 Hz during major geomagnetic storms, but the decadal mean sits within ±0.1 Hz. A model whose own geometry forbids the observed frequency cannot claim credit for that frequency remaining stable; stability of a value you cannot produce is not a confirmation, and both the globe and the dome would predict stability of any resonance their cavity supports — stability is not a discriminating test.
detail_verdict_text: Dome cavity formula predicts ~22 Hz baseline; globe ionospheric physics explains 7.83 Hz stability. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The dome claims prospective status via OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring — and the OTS infrastructure is real. However, OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file (see Timestamp Error). The Schumann fundamental resonance at 7.83 Hz was theoretically predicted by Schumann (1952) and first measured by König & Schumann (1954); it has been continuously monitored since the 1960s (Sentman 1987; Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002). Registering the claim that 7.83 Hz "remains stable" 74 years after Schumann's 1952 theoretical prediction and timestamping it with OTS does not make it a prediction — it makes it a crypto-wrapped textbook fact. The Part 3.1b standard requires data "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for a 74-year-old measurement.
detail_extra: Timestamp note: This WIN is claimed as prospective via OpenTimestamps — but OTS anchors the observation file, not the prediction. See Timestamp Error.
tldr_evidence: The dome's own height formula H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km has its maximum at r = 0, giving a minimum quarter-wave frequency of 8.78 Hz at the pole and rising monotonically outward (27 Hz at r = 10,000 km, ~90 Hz at the rim). 7.83 Hz sits below that floor at every single point, so no averaging scheme can rescue it. Standard ionospheric physics already explains 7.83 Hz via Earth's 40,075 km circumference with D-layer damping.
tldr_verdict: The dome's own cavity cannot produce 7.83 Hz at any radius — 8.78 Hz is its pole-floor minimum, and the frequency only rises from there. Standard globe ionospheric physics explains the observed frequency and its stability.

finding: Ocean tidal dynamo (seawater + globe magnetic field) explains signal; dome has no ocean induction mechanism
detail_claim: Lunar magnetic field is 1–2 nanoTesla, confirming dome model.
detail_evidence: The ocean tidal dynamo — seawater moving through Earth's magnetic field — generates the 1–2 nT lunar magnetic signal first detected by CHAMP (Tyler et al. 2003, Science 299:239) and refined by Swarm (Sabaka et al. 2015). Standard electromagnetic induction predicts the magnitude, spatial pattern (strongest over deep ocean basins with fast tidal currents), and phase from known seawater conductivity (~3.5 S/m) and tidal velocities — specificity the dome model cannot match. Crucially, the globe model predicts both the magnitude (~1–2 nT at satellite altitude) and the spatial distribution (signal strongest over deep ocean basins such as the Indian Ocean and South Atlantic, where tidal currents are fast and water-column depth is greatest; nearly absent over continents) from first principles: σ_seawater × v_tidal × B_main. The dome model cannot predict this spatial pattern because flat-disc geometry with a local moon at 2,534 km would concentrate tidal forcing directly beneath the moon's circuit path — not over specific ocean basins. Crucially, the globe model predicts both the magnitude (~1–2 nT at satellite altitude) and the spatial distribution (signal strongest over deep ocean basins such as the Indian Ocean and South Atlantic, where tidal currents are fast and water-column depth is greatest; nearly absent over continents) from first principles: σ_seawater × v_tidal × B_main. The dome model cannot predict this spatial pattern because flat-disc geometry with a local moon at 2,534 km would concentrate tidal forcing directly beneath the moon's circuit path — not over specific ocean basins. Notably, the dome's own prediction (W015: −1.0 nT ±0.5) disagrees in sign with its claimed confirmation (1–2 nT positive) — the dome predicts a negative excursion (field weakening), but the observation shows a positive enhancement. The sign error is unexplained and unaddressed. The measurement was published over 20 years before the dome model claimed it as a prediction.
detail_verdict_text: The signal is generated by seawater flowing through Earth's magnetic field (electromagnetic induction). The dome model has no ocean induction mechanism — its 'aetheric' field does not interact with seawater conductivity. The signal's magnitude and phase match globe predictions precisely. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The dome claims prospective status via OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring — and the OTS infrastructure is real. However, OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file (see Timestamp Error). The 1–2 nT lunar magnetic signal was published by Tyler et al. (2003) in Science and refined by Sabaka et al. (2015) using Swarm satellite data — over 20 years before the dome model claimed it as a prediction. The dome's own predicted value (W015: −1.0 nT ±0.5) disagrees in sign with its claimed confirmation (1–2 nT positive); the dome does not resolve this sign/magnitude discrepancy and provides no phase, station, or temporal qualifier that would allow independent reproduction of the measurement. The Part 3.1b standard requires data "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for measurements published two decades prior.
detail_extra: Timestamp note: This WIN is claimed as prospective via OpenTimestamps — but OTS anchors the observation file, not the prediction. See Timestamp Error.
tldr_evidence: The 1-2 nT lunar magnetic signal is generated by ocean tidal currents flowing through Earth's magnetic field — electromagnetic induction first detected by satellite (Tyler et al. 2003) and confirmed by ESA's Swarm mission. The dome has no ocean induction mechanism.
tldr_verdict: The signal comes from seawater flowing through Earth's magnetic field — real electromagnetic induction. The dome has no ocean induction mechanism and no derivation.

finding: Non-discriminating: dome observes position from same data as WMM; no unique prediction derived from dome geometry
detail_claim: SAA western cell remains west of 45°W longitude.
detail_evidence:
The SAA western cell has a centre near 65–75°W drifting westward at 0.2–0.3°/yr (NOAA WMM 2025; Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Front. Earth Sci. 4:40). The 45°W threshold sits ~20–30° east of the current cell centre — the cell would have to reverse direction and travel 20–30° east to falsify it, with no historical precedent for such a reversal on century timescales. W022 is therefore a description of current geography with a >100-year safety margin, not a falsifiable prediction. More fundamentally, the dome's primary geometric (firmament-height) function H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on radial distance r from the Axis Mundi and has zero azimuthal derivative — so longitude-specific SAA features (American cell ~80°W, African cell ~30°E, any specific western-boundary longitude) cannot be derived from dome geometry at all; they must be imported from WMM/CHAOS-7 output. Standard MHD does not pre-register a specific 1900-vintage SAA longitude either, but it does derive the two-cell morphology, westward drift, and current positions from first-principles core-dynamo dynamics over reversed-flux patches at the core–mantle boundary (Terra-Nova & Amit 2017, Phys. Earth Planet. Inter. 266:39–53); the dome adopts the end state without a derivation path.

These 9 WINs draw from the same INTERMAGNET, NOAA, and Tomsk datasets already used by WIN-004 through WIN-039. Each takes a single data point or ratio from existing measurements and declares it a new 'WIN.' For example, WIN-042 (field decay >=28 nT/year) uses the same threshold and data as WIN-037 (field decay >=28 nT). WIN-043 (NMP drift 2.26x longitudinal) was already cited in WIN-007's evidence. Notably, the dome's own predictions tracker listed W022 (the prediction corresponding to WIN-040) as PENDING in our raw-text snapshot (2026-03-12), meaning the dome itself had not confirmed this WIN at baseline. WIN-061 (Schumann suppression during G3 storms) documents that ionospheric disturbance during geomagnetic storms affects Schumann resonance, which is standard ionospheric physics documented since the 1960s.
detail_verdict_text: All are either duplicates of earlier WINs or standard geophysical observations already documented in the literature. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value for WIN-040 comes from NOAA's WMM-2025 and ESA's CHAOS-7 SAA cell-position outputs, publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the 45°W threshold in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
tldr_evidence: These 9 WINs (040–043, 059–061, 063 batch) draw from the same INTERMAGNET, NOAA, and Tomsk datasets already used by earlier WINs. Each takes a single data point or ratio from existing measurements and declares it a new confirmation. They are either duplicates or well-known geophysical observations.
tldr_verdict: All are duplicates of earlier WINs or standard observations from the same datasets. No new physics, no new data.

finding: Multi-station observation of a single regional anomaly (SAA) is one confirmation, not five independent ones; same INTERMAGNET data as WIN-004/005/035/037
detail_claim: SAA exhibits multi-station magnetic decay exceeding 28 nT/year.
detail_evidence: WIN-041 reprocesses INTERMAGNET SAA-region station data — the same observatory network, station coverage, and time windows used across WIN-004, WIN-005, WIN-035, and WIN-037. These WINs cite overlapping stations and identical time windows. The decay rates are real physical observations, but counting the same dataset five times as five separate confirmations inflates the win count without introducing new evidence. A further circularity: the dome's own monitoring code hardcodes the validation threshold at ≥28 nT/year — a value not derived from any dome-geometry equation but manually set in the model's configuration. Confirming that the observed SAA decay rate exceeds a threshold the model itself defined is not an independent prediction.
detail_verdict_text: WIN-041 draws from the same INTERMAGNET network as WIN-004/005/035/037. Even when different stations are cited, multiple SAA stations within the anomaly’s ~5,000–8,000 km spatial correlation length are not independent measurements — they are correlated samples of a single geophysical anomaly. Claiming each station as a separate confirmation is equivalent to counting each thermometer in a warm room as independent proof the room is warm. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
tldr_evidence: WIN-041 reprocesses INTERMAGNET station data from the same observatory network used in WIN-004, 005, 035, and 037. Multiple SAA stations within the anomaly's 5,000-8,000 km correlation length are not independent measurements — they're measuring the same regional phenomenon.
tldr_verdict: WIN-041 draws from the same stations as multiple earlier WINs. Stations within the SAA's spatial correlation length aren't independent — this is one observation counted multiple times.

finding: Mathematically equivalent to WIN-037 (≥28 nT cumulative per 12 months = ≥28 nT/yr rate); both WINs test SAA-core stations only, not the global dipole — see WIN-037 for full analysis
detail_claim: Global magnetic field decays at rate ≥28 nT/year.
detail_evidence: WIN-037 (PROS-007) claims the global field decayed by ≥28 nT in 12 months. WIN-042 claims the ongoing decay rate is ≥28 nT/year. These are mathematically equivalent: if the field dropped ≥28 nT in any 12-month window, the rate was ≥28 nT/year over that window. Both share the same fundamental problem: the 28 nT/yr threshold is met only at SAA-core stations (Tsumeb: ~30 nT/yr), not globally (~15 nT/yr from IGRF dipole moment decline). The dome's monitoring code checks only regional stations, confirming both WINs test the same regional measurement. Both use the same NOAA World Magnetic Model and INTERMAGNET observatory data. The ≥28 nT/yr threshold is met at SAA-core stations (regional decay of 20–35 nT/yr per NCEI/WMM) but the global dipole moment is declining at ~5% per century, approximately 15 nT/yr in globally averaged surface intensity (Thébault et al., 2015, IGRF-12) — well below the 28 nT/yr threshold. The dome does not specify whether it means global or regional, allowing it to claim whichever meets the threshold. Duplicating the same claim across two WIN IDs does not create two independent confirmations.
detail_verdict_text: WIN-042 duplicates WIN-037; identical data and threshold cannot justify two separate WINs. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
tldr_evidence: WIN-042 claims an ongoing decay rate of ≥28 nT/year. WIN-037 already claimed the field decayed by ≥28 nT in 12 months. These are mathematically identical statements — you can't count the same measurement as two separate predictions.
tldr_verdict: WIN-042 is mathematically identical to WIN-037. Same data, same threshold — one observation counted twice.

A number extracted from a single unspecified snapshot of a non-stationary trajectory is a historical description, not a stable falsifiable prediction.

claim: Firmament Scaling Function from V12 geometry
finding: Formula output is version-unstable: HAD FSF computes to 1.47 under V51 parameters but 1.89 under V50.6, and the BOU reference radius shifts from 5,715 km to 9,983 km between dome versions — a 28% discrepancy in the same station's FSF value, self-contradicting the 'derived from V12 geometry' claim
detail_claim: Firmament Scaling Function (FSF) derives from Version 12 dome geometry.
detail_evidence:
The FSF formula's output is version-unstable: under V51 parameters (ellipse H(r), V51 station radii) the published values (EBR 1.029, HAD 1.471, LER 2.075, etc.) reproduce to within ~3%, but under V50.6 parameters (exponential H(r), V50.6 radii) the same formula produces HAD 1.89 — a 28% discrepancy at a single station. The BOU reference station that anchors the ratio moved from r = 5,715 km (V50.6) to r = 9,983 km (V51). A formula genuinely derived from V12 geometry should not swing 28% when the author revises other parameters; WIN-044's detail_claim asserts V12 derivation, but the reproduction profile shows the formula is tied to V51-specific parameters. A second structural contradiction is documented in Section 1.8: dome disc-radii are back-computed from Polaris elevation angles, making any formula that uses those radii — including FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) — parasitic on globe coordinates.

The eclipse predictions (E001–E009) that would independently test the FSF formula against INTERMAGNET station data cannot occur until the August 12, 2026 eclipse. Until then, this WIN confirms nothing beyond internal algebraic consistency — the formula is treated as 'confirmed' because it was derived from dome parameters, not because it was tested against nature.stency.

Direct dome source: check_eclipse_fsf.py defines FSF as (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) — purely geometric. Station-specific amplitudes are produced by plugging dome-coordinate station radii and the dome H(r) profile into this ratio. The formula does not take eclipse magnitude as input; coverage is applied separately as ΔB = baseline × coverage × FSF in monitor.py Domain 10 (line 515). The published E001–E009 FSF values (EBR 1.029, HAD 1.471, LER 2.075, …) reproduce to within ~3% under the V51 ellipse H(r) with V51 station radii (check_fsf.py), but do NOT reproduce under the V50.6 exponential H(r) with V50.6 radii (HAD computes to 1.89 vs published 1.47). The baseline station radii move between dome versions: BOU shifts from r=5715 (V50.6) to r=9983 (V51).
detail_verdict_text: The FSF formula self-contradicts: the same station (HAD) produces FSF = 1.47 under V51 parameters and FSF = 1.89 under V50.6 — a 28% discrepancy driven by the BOU reference radius shifting from 5,715 km to 9,983 km between dome versions. A formula whose output changes by 28% when the author revises their own model is not a stable geometric derivation; it is parameter-fitted. Section 1.8's coordinate-collapse argument adds a second structural contradiction: the station radii input to FSF are back-computed from Polaris elevation angles observed through the same n(r) aetheric medium the formula relies on.

code_analysis.post_hoc_evidence: Firmament Scaling Function (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU). Eclipse predictions registered before August 2026 test window. Formula is version-unstable: HAD FSF = 1.47 (V51) vs 1.89 (V50.6); BOU reference radius shifts 5,715 → 9,983 km between versions. The derivation claim is undermined by version-instability.
code_analysis.monitoring_evidence: No domain fetches INTERMAGNET or any eclipse magnetometer data. The E001–E009 numbers are hardcoded text in 03-predictions.txt. monitor.py Domain 10 is referenced as the consumer of FSF but no live fetch exists against the August 2026 eclipse because that event has not occurred. 'none' is correct.
code_analysis.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard ionospheric physics explains eclipse magnetic perturbations via Sq current modulation driven by latitude-dependent E-region conductivity changes. The dome's FSF is a geometric ratio with no standard counterpart. The relabel concern is softer: FSF(r) may incidentally correlate with geomagnetic latitude, so an apparent 'match' to eclipse data could reflect standard-physics latitude structure rather than dome geometry — but that is a causal-confound critique, not a relabel. 'false' is correct.
code_analysis.derives_from_dome_evidence: Structurally yes: FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU), with H(r) = 8537 × exp(−r/8619) (V12 parameters). A derivation path from dome parameters to station amplitudes exists. However, the r_k values for each station come from r·tan(θ_Polaris) = H(r), which Section 1.8 shows depends on Polaris elevation through the n(r) medium — the same medium used to fix the sun-altitude collision. The coordinate system that assigns station radii is contaminated by the refraction fix.

tldr_evidence: The FSF formula produces different values under different dome parameter sets: HAD computes to 1.47 under V51 parameters but 1.89 under V50.6, a 28% discrepancy. The BOU reference radius — a foundation of the formula — shifts from 5,715 km to 9,983 km between dome versions. This version-instability means the 'derived from V12 geometry' claim is self-contradicted by the author's own parameter revisions.
tldr_verdict: The FSF formula self-contradicts: 28% variation in HAD's predicted value between dome versions. Formulas derived from fixed geometry should give fixed outputs — this one does not.

finding: Period is trivial (half a lunar day); dome's local moon produces one sharp tidal spike, not the observed two-bulge pattern
detail_claim: Five new WINs confirm that tidal constituent periods (M2, S2, K1, O1, N2) match dome predictions.
detail_evidence: The M2 tidal period (12.42 hours) is half a lunar day (24.84h / 2). Any model that uses a 24.84-hour lunar circuit inherently produces M2 by division — this is a tautology, not a prediction. But the deeper problem is the tidal pattern, not just the period. The dome model places the moon at ~2,534 km altitude. On the globe (moon at 384,400 km = 60× Earth's radius), the tidal force varies by only ~6.6% across Earth's diameter (first-order gravitational force approximation; tidal differential force varies by ~10%), producing two nearly equal tidal bulges — one toward the moon, one away. This is why every coastal city sees two high tides per lunar day. On the dome's flat disc (radius ~20,015 km), with the moon only 2,534 km above, the tidal force is a sharp spike directly beneath the moon: at 5,000 km offset it drops to 9% of peak; at the equator (~14,000 km) it's 0.6% of peak. There is no far-side bulge because the far edge of the disc is 8× farther from the moon than the sub-lunar point. The dome predicts one tidal pulse per day (when the moon passes overhead), not the observed two. Standard tidal theory (Doodson, 1921) derives hundreds of tidal constituents (389 in the full harmonic expansion; 62 is a commonly cited practical subset), including amplitudes and the two-bulge pattern, from a distant moon. See Section 2.2.
detail_verdict_text: Tidal periods are trivial (half a lunar day). The dome's local moon produces one sharp spike, not the observed global two-bulge pattern with two equal high tides per day. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test on structural grounds. The numerical value is textbook physics (Part 3.1b, "trivial-transcription test"): it appears in undergraduate references and widely indexed resources, so there is no meaningful sense in which the dome monitor script "predicted" it before reading the data. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call, making even that weaker test unverifiable. No legitimate-novelty carve-out applies: the value is not obscure and the derivation (where one is offered at all) is algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation. See Part 3.1b.
tldr_evidence: The M2 tidal period (12.42 hours) is simply half a lunar day — any model with a ~24.84-hour lunar circuit gets this by division. But the dome's nearby moon at 2,534 km altitude would produce a single sharp tidal spike directly underneath it, not the observed pattern of two equal high tides per day seen globally.
tldr_verdict: The period is trivial (half a lunar day). The dome's local moon produces one spike, not the two-bulge global pattern actually observed.

finding: Dome claims low-z deviations support aetheric redshift, but provides no photon-aether interaction equation or redshift mechanism
detail_claim: Low-redshift Hubble Law deviations support aetheric redshift model.
detail_evidence: Hubble's Law (v = H₀·d) is one of the most precisely confirmed relationships in cosmology, verified from z = 0.001 to z = 2.26 with 1,701 supernovae (see Pantheon+ supernova compilation), extended to z ~ 8 with gamma-ray bursts. The dome model has no mechanism for generating redshift — no photon-aether interaction theory, no propagation equations — and no explanation for the observed isotropy of Hubble Law across all directions. The most fundamental obstacle is ontological: the dome's firmament extends to ~9,086 km, yet galaxies are observed at millions of light-years. The dome model provides no spatial framework for extragalactic objects — without one, any claim about their redshift behavior is physically undefined. A further problem: if aetheric propagation causes redshift, then the dome's model must predict a DIFFERENT Hubble constant than standard cosmology — the value of H₀ would encode the aetheric attenuation rate per unit distance, not the cosmic expansion rate. The dome makes no such prediction, instead adopting the same H₀ range (67–74 km/s/Mpc) used by standard cosmology. If the mechanism differs but the prediction is identical, the observation confirms neither mechanism. Note also that the dome invokes "low-z deviations" as evidence, but the Hubble tension — H₀ = 73.04 km/s/Mpc (SH0ES, Cepheid-calibrated) vs. 67.4 km/s/Mpc (Planck CMB), a >5σ discrepancy — is a genuine unresolved problem in standard cosmology. Both competing measurements use standard physics; the dome predicts neither value and offers no mechanism for the tension's resolution. Pointing at genuine anomalies in standard cosmology does not validate an alternative model that provides no different numerical prediction.
detail_verdict_text: H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc is measured from standard cosmology. The dome model adopts this value without derivation. It has no photon-aether interaction equation, no redshift mechanism, and no galaxy-scale physics. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test on structural grounds. The numerical value is textbook physics (Part 3.1b, "trivial-transcription test"): it appears in undergraduate references and widely indexed resources, so there is no meaningful sense in which the dome monitor script "predicted" it before reading the data. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call, making even that weaker test unverifiable. No legitimate-novelty carve-out applies: the value is not obscure and the derivation (where one is offered at all) is algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation. See Part 3.1b.
tldr_evidence: Hubble's Law (galaxies recede faster the farther away they are) is confirmed from nearby galaxies to billions of light-years away using 1,700+ supernovae. The dome adopts H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc without any derivation. It has no photon-aether interaction equation, no redshift mechanism, and no galaxy-scale physics.
tldr_verdict: The dome adopts the standard Hubble constant without derivation. It has no redshift mechanism and no physics that operates at galaxy scales.

finding: False dilemma (ΛCDM problem ≠ dome evidence); anomaly significance eroded by Planck reanalysis; copper firmament (WIN-034) would block CMB microwaves
detail_claim: Cosmic Microwave Background Axis of Evil supports dome model.
detail_evidence:
The dome categorizes this as a 'ΛCDM Counter-Prediction' — arguing not that the dome predicts the Axis of Evil, but that ΛCDM cannot explain it. This is a false dilemma: even if the Axis of Evil were a genuine ΛCDM anomaly, this would not constitute evidence for the dome. Dozens of competing cosmological models could potentially address CMB anomalies while remaining consistent with the broader observational record.

The anomaly itself has diminished in significance. Planck 2015 Results XXIII found alignment p-values range from 0.5% to 5% depending on statistical estimator, sky mask, and multipole range — with some tests yielding non-significant results. The look-elsewhere effect (testing many CMB statistical properties and reporting the anomalous ones) further erodes significance. Planck 2018 isotropy and statistics analysis confirmed these conclusions: low-multipole anomalies remain equivocal, with significance varying from ~1% to non-significant depending on estimator choice and sky coverage. Researchers have additionally noted that the quadrupole-octopole alignment is sensitive to Galactic plane foreground masking — the apparent cosmic alignment partially reflects the geometry of pixels excluded near the Galactic plane rather than an intrinsic cosmological signal.

Self-contradiction with WIN-034: The CMB at 160.2 GHz peak frequency is observed uniformly from all directions — including directly overhead, through the alleged firmament. If the firmament is 'cast copper/bronze' (WIN-034), it should be completely opaque to microwaves (copper skin depth at 160 GHz is ~0.2 μm). The dome model faces a dilemma: either the firmament is microwave-transparent (contradicting WIN-034) or the CMB cannot be observed at all (contradicting WIN-048's premise).

The dome model provides no mechanism for CMB generation or propagation in a flat geometry. A dome defender might reinterpret the CMB as thermal emission from the firmament itself at 2.725 K — but a uniform dome surface at that temperature would produce a featureless blackbody spectrum with none of the observed acoustic peak structure (first peak at multipole ℓ ≈ 220), E-mode polarization, or gravitational lensing signal. These features require photon-baryon fluid oscillations at z ≈ 1100, a physical history with no analog in dome geometry.
detail_verdict_text: The 'ΛCDM Counter-Prediction' framing is a false dilemma. The Axis of Evil's significance has eroded under Planck reanalysis (p-values 0.5%–5%). The dome's copper firmament (WIN-034) would be completely opaque to the 160 GHz CMB — a direct self-contradiction with observing the CMB at all. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
detail_extra:
Update (2026-04-10): The dome author revised the WIN-048 confidence claim on 2026-03-23, acknowledging that the original ">99.9% confirmed by two missions" framing overstated the Planck full-mission result. The dome site now states verbatim: "Planck full-mission significance is 2–3σ — not the >99.9% originally reported by WMAP. The claim ‘confirmed at >99.9% by two missions’ overstated the Planck result. Confidence level corrected accordingly." The entry further notes that "the 2–3σ anomaly continues to favour ECM structurally while remaining below the threshold for definitive confirmation" and classifies the result as CONSISTENT rather than confirmed, with a caveat that galactic masking artefacts account for ~20% of simulated alignments.

Kernel of truth: this is a responsible self-correction and we acknowledge it. The author voluntarily demoted a headline confidence number after the Planck reanalysis rather than hide behind the older WMAP figure, which is more intellectual honesty than most advocacy sites manage. On that narrow process point, the dome author gets it right.

But the correction does not rescue WIN-048. Three reasons our verdict stands:
  1. The false dilemma survives unchanged. Whether the Axis of Evil is a 6σ or 2σ anomaly, "ΛCDM struggles with this" is not evidence for a dome. The dome’s own WIN-048 entry still frames this as a "ΛCDM Counter-Prediction" — a single-bit negative signal about a competing model cannot distinguish a flat dome from dozens of alternative cosmologies that also accommodate CMB anomalies.
  2. 2–3σ lands exactly where our rebuttal already put it. We cited Planck 2015 Results XXIII (alignment p-values 0.5%–5% depending on estimator and mask) and Planck 2018 isotropy results. The dome’s new self-assessment of 2–3σ (p ≈ 0.3%–5%) sits inside the same range, so the corrected dome claim is now numerically compatible with the mainstream Planck consensus — the "anomaly" the dome still leans on is the same equivocal low-multipole oddity Planck itself treats as look-elsewhere sensitive.
  3. The WIN-034 self-contradiction is untouched. Downgrading confidence in the CMB alignment does nothing to resolve the geometric problem we raised: a cast copper/bronze firmament (WIN-034) has a ~0.2 μm skin depth at 160 GHz and would be completely opaque to the CMB. Either the firmament is microwave-transparent (contradicting WIN-034) or there is no CMB to have an Axis of Evil in (contradicting WIN-048’s premise). Lowering the statistical significance does not change the opacity of copper.
Net effect on verdict: "Misleading" still applies. The weakened claim is less misleading than the original, and we credit the author for the correction, but the structural problems (false dilemma, self-contradiction with WIN-034, no dome mechanism for CMB generation in flat geometry) are independent of the σ-value and remain fatal.
tldr_evidence: The dome argues that the 'Axis of Evil' (apparent alignment of CMB features) disproves standard cosmology, therefore supports the dome. This is a false dilemma — even if the alignment were real, it wouldn't support a 20,000 km flat disc. Under Planck reanalysis, the statistical significance has weakened substantially.
tldr_verdict: The 'ΛCDM Counter-Prediction' framing is a false dilemma. The Axis of Evil's significance has eroded under Planck reanalysis, and a copper firmament would block the CMB observations entirely.
