finding: Shadow zone at 104-140 deg proves spherical layered Earth with liquid core
detail_claim: The seismic P-wave shadow zone at 104°-140° from an earthquake epicenter matches dome geometry.
detail_evidence: The P-wave shadow zone is one of the most powerful pieces of evidence FOR a spherical, layered Earth. Seismic waves refract as they pass through layers of different density. The shadow zone at 104°–140° was first explained by Oldham (1906) and refined by Gutenberg (1913, Physikalische Zeitschrift 14: 1217–1218) as proof that Earth has a liquid outer core. S-waves are completely blocked by the outer core (they cannot propagate through liquid), creating a total S-wave shadow beyond ~104°. P-waves refract around the core and re-emerge as PKP arrivals beyond ~140°, with the shadow zone between these angles. The radius and depth of the liquid core (2,891 km depth, outer core from 2,891–5,150 km) are derived directly from this shadow zone geometry on a SPHERE. On a flat disc 20,015 km in radius, angular distances of 104°–140° (corresponding to 11,600–15,600 km surface distance, using sphere math — which is the point) have no geometric relationship to a layered spherical refraction pattern. Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating: the shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers. The azimuthal-symmetry problem. The dome names a specific mechanism: its WIN-064 entry states that the Sub-Terrestrial Wall of the Closed Toroidal Ovoid creates a physical P-wave shadow zone between 104° and 140° angular distance from any seismic source, corresponding to the toroidal return-path boundary. This mechanism is a fixed geographic feature (the bottom plate of the ovoid cavity). A fixed boundary cannot reproduce the observed geometry. The P-wave shadow zone forms a symmetric ring at 104°–140° around every earthquake, at every azimuth, regardless of where the epicenter sits — Mount Aso yesterday, the Chile Triple Junction today, Tohoku tomorrow. A shadow cast by a single fixed wall would fall in specific directions relative to that wall (east-of-the-wall, west-of-the-wall), not form a concentric ring around each new epicenter worldwide. The standard-model explanation produces observer-centered rings by construction, because the spherical core is concentric with the radial projection of each epicenter. See Part 6 for the broader pattern of observer-centered geometry that any fixed-boundary mechanism inherently fails. The live-power panel is measuring the wrong thing. The dome's monitor surfaces this WIN as Domain 14 on its live-power dashboard with predicted: "Shadow at 104–140°", observed: "Latest deep quake: 15 km SSE of Sant'Angelo, Italy (depth=378.035 km)", tolerance_pct: null, error_pct: null, pass: true (current status_history.json, domain index 14). Three problems in one panel: (1) the observation is a live-fetched USGS record of a recent deep earthquake — its focal depth in kilometres — which has no logical connection to whether P-waves are absent at 104°–140° angular distance from that quake; (2) pass: true is set unconditionally because the observation is never compared to the prediction; (3) the dome's own methodology states Each domain has explicit tolerance bands. If prediction falls outside tolerance, it fails — no exceptions, yet this domain ships with null tolerance. A domain with null tolerance that never actually compares obs to pred is unfalsifiable by the dome's own self-imposed criterion and should not count toward the accuracy denominator.
detail_verdict_text: The shadow zone proves spherical layered Earth; its very derivation assumes a sphere. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test on structural grounds. The numerical value is textbook physics (Part 3.1b, "trivial-transcription test"): it appears in undergraduate references and widely indexed resources, so there is no meaningful sense in which the dome monitor script "predicted" it before reading the data. Under the dome author's own criterion (“Timestamped before confirming data”), that framing doesn’t even apply here: the dome lists no live fetch for this domain at all (monitoring: hardcoded) — the 104–140 deg “observation” is a static string in monitor.py, so there is no data-pull event to timestamp. No legitimate-novelty carve-out applies: the value is not obscure and the derivation (where one is offered at all) is algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation. See Part 3.1b.
detail_extra:
Preempting the "radially symmetric structure" defense. A dome defender could try to rescue azimuthal symmetry by arguing the Sub-Terrestrial Wall is itself radially symmetric about the disc center — for instance, a concentric annular feature on the bottom plate. This does not help. Symmetry about a single fixed point on the disc is not the same as symmetry about every earthquake's epicenter. A concentric annulus centered on the disc pole would produce shadows at 104°–140° measured from the disc pole — not from every epicenter. For observer-centered rings around every quake worldwide, the sub-terrestrial structure would have to be radially symmetric about a different center for each quake — i.e., there would have to be an infinite family of concentric layered bodies, one per possible epicenter, all simultaneously present. The only geometric object that satisfies "radially symmetric about every surface point simultaneously" is a concentric spherical shell beneath a spherical surface. Conceding this defense is therefore equivalent to conceding the sphere. The more honest dome reply is to drop the "confirmed" label and restate WIN-064 as "disc cosmology is not logically incompatible with shadow zones" — but a model that is merely not-incompatible with an observation makes no prediction, and the dome's own scoreboard counts this as a confirmation, not a non-contradiction.

Closing the “non-incompatibility” retreat (preemptive). The strongest remaining dome rebuttal is to step back from mechanism and argue that WIN-064 was never a derivation claim — the disc cosmology is simply not incompatible with the observed shadow zone, so a confirmation is warranted whether or not any disc geometry produces 104°–140°. This retreat is unavailable while the scoreboard reads as currently written, and ineffective even if it is taken. Unavailable: the public WIN-064 entry commits to a specific causal mechanism — “the Sub-Terrestrial Wall of the Closed Toroidal Ovoid creates a physical P-wave shadow zone between 104° and 140°… corresponding to the toroidal return-path boundary,” and the Dome-vs-Globe row explicitly re-asserts the mechanism as a physical structural boundary … where the aetheric return path meets the bottom plate of the ovoid cavity. “Creates” is a production claim, not a compatibility claim; invoking non-incompatibility would require the author to publicly strike the verb, delete the mechanism sentence, and demote the CONFIRMED badge to “not logically forbidden by our geometry.” Ineffective: the dome’s own methodology, quoted verbatim on the live-power dashboard, states Each domain has explicit tolerance bands. If prediction falls outside tolerance, it fails — no exceptions. A non-incompatibility claim has no tolerance band by construction — every observation is compatible with “maybe, maybe not” — so every observation passes. That is already visibly the state of this panel (Tolerance: N/A, Observed in kilometres of focal depth while Predicted is in degrees of angular distance, Status: PASS regardless). By the dome’s own self-imposed criterion, the retreat converts this domain into an unfalsifiable free-PASS that should not count toward the accuracy denominator — alongside any other N/A-tolerance domain that similarly lacks a threshold or categorical criterion (threshold-style panels such as “Anomaly >2σ” are not affected by this critique). The dome cannot hold the “94.5% accuracy” figure and the non-incompatibility framing simultaneously: either WIN-064 carries a quantitative prediction with a tolerance (in which case the depth-vs-angle category mismatch and missing tolerance are unresolved failures), or it is a compatibility statement (in which case it is withdrawn from the confirmed count, along with any other N/A-tolerance domain that similarly lacks a threshold or categorical criterion). The same disjunction closes the fallback reading of Tolerance: N/A = deterministic yes/no: a genuinely qualitative test would omit the numbers and accept a shadow at any angle as confirmation, but the dome keeps the 104°–140° boundary on the scoreboard. You cannot ask for quantitative credit and qualitative falsifiability immunity on the same line.
tldr_evidence: The P-wave shadow zone (no direct P-waves between 104°–140° from an earthquake) is one of the strongest proofs of a spherical, layered Earth. Crucially it forms a symmetric ring around every earthquake worldwide — a pattern a fixed "Sub-Terrestrial Wall" cannot produce, but concentric spherical layers do naturally. The dome adopts the observation while proposing a mechanism geometrically incompatible with what's observed.
tldr_verdict: The shadow zone proves a spherical, layered Earth — its derivation assumes a sphere. Claiming it as dome evidence is self-defeating.

finding: Polaris is 0.74° from true pole; 'excess' is within known offset. See Kill-Shot Test 2 (no error budget provided)
detail_claim: Polaris elevation at 35.9°N systematically exceeds latitude by +0.27°, confirming dome height function.
detail_evidence: Polaris (Alpha Ursae Minoris) is not at the north celestial pole — its declination is ~89.26° (2026), so it traces a circle of radius ~0.74° around the true pole. At any given time, Polaris can appear up to 0.74° above or below true north. The claimed +0.27° ‘systematic excess’ at 35.9°N is well within this 0.74° envelope and is not systematic at all — it depends on the time of observation during Polaris’s diurnal circle. Of 24 cities in the dome’s own coordinate table, 23 match WGS84 latitude to within ±0.05°, consistent with the globe model. The dome’s own table lists Oslo Polaris elevation at 59.90° (WGS84: 59.91°N), showing essentially zero excess — directly contradicting PRED-NEW-001’s prediction of a systematic multi-degree elevation excess at high latitudes. Additionally, atmospheric refraction at mid-latitudes adds ~0.02–0.03° at typical Polaris elevations, and the dome provides no error budget (instrument precision, atmospheric conditions, number of measurements, time of observation). The combined error budget — Polaris's 0.74° polar offset (time-dependent), ~0.02–0.03° atmospheric refraction at mid-latitudes, and ±0.2–0.5° typical measurement uncertainty — yields a total uncertainty band of ±0.3–0.5°. The claimed +0.27° 'excess' falls entirely within this band and is not statistically distinguishable from zero. A single unreplicated measurement with no stated uncertainty, no time-of-observation record, and no instrument calibration is not a confirmation. Centuries of precision astrometry — from Tycho Brahe through Hipparcos and Gaia — confirm that true celestial pole altitude equals observer latitude to arcsecond precision using proper pole-star corrections. The dome's PRED-NEW-001, registered 2026-03-12, appeared after extensive site development work and uses Polaris observations published in standard star catalogs for decades — this is retrodiction of known data, not prospective prediction. Notably, the dome's broader prediction 'Polaris altitude matches latitude' is listed as FALSIFIED on the dome's own site (2026-03-15) — the model cannot simultaneously claim both falsification and confirmation of related predictions. The refractive index implied by the dome’s H(r) function — n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) — highlights a structural dilemma detailed in Section 1.8: any n(r) strong enough to produce the multi-degree refraction needed for PRED-NEW-001’s claimed +4.5° excess at Oslo also contaminates the H(r) coordinate system that calibrated the dome’s distances. The dome cannot invoke strong refraction to explain PRED-NEW-001 while keeping weak refraction to preserve coordinate accuracy.
detail_verdict_text: Polaris is 0.74° from true pole; the claimed +0.27° 'excess' is within the known offset range. No error budget is provided (atmospheric refraction, time-of-night, instrument precision). See Kill-Shot Test 2 for the full analysis showing the dome's own site contradicts this claim.
tldr_evidence: Polaris isn't exactly at the north celestial pole — it's ~0.74° off and traces a small circle. At any given time, Polaris can appear up to 0.74° above or below its mean position. The dome claims a systematic +0.27° 'excess,' but that's well within the known offset range. No error budget accounts for atmospheric refraction, time-of-night, or instrument precision.
tldr_verdict: The claimed +0.27° excess is within Polaris's known 0.74° offset from true pole. No error budget is provided — refraction, timing, and instrument precision could all account for it.

finding: Budget is remarkably symmetric (Stephens 2012); residual from NH land fraction; dome has no radiation budget model
detail_claim: Dome predicts persistent Northern Hemisphere energy absorption excess of +0.34 W/m² (static), claimed to confirm dome geometric asymmetry. The actual observation is a decadal trend in Earth's energy imbalance — not a static hemispheric feature.
detail_evidence: Earth's hemispheric energy balance is remarkably symmetric — a key finding of Stephens et al. (2012, Nature Geoscience). Small residual differences arise from the northern hemisphere having ~39% land vs ~19% in the south, producing differential thermal inertia, albedo, and evaporation rates. The +0.34 W/m² figure cited by the dome is a decadal trend in Earth's energy imbalance (Raghuraman et al., 2025, PNAS), driven by aerosol changes, Arctic ice loss, and cloud shifts — not a static hemispheric difference. While the dome's disc geometry is inherently asymmetric (NP at center, varying firmament height), no radiation budget calculation on the dome site translates this geometry into a specific W/m² prediction. Geometric asymmetry is not a quantitative prediction. Moreover, dome geometry itself predicts a far larger hemispheric energy difference: a flat disc with a local sun at 5,733 km altitude creates extreme inverse-square flux falloff. Integration over the disc shows the inner half (analogous to the northern hemisphere) receives roughly 2.3× the solar energy of the outer half — predicting a hemispheric flux difference on the order of 90–270 W/m². This is hundreds of times larger than the 0.34 W/m² static difference the dome claims as confirmation, meaning dome geometry is not just uncorroborated — it actively predicts the wrong magnitude by several orders of magnitude.
detail_verdict_text: Stephens et al. (2012, Nature Geoscience) showed Earth's hemispheric energy balance is remarkably symmetric. The +0.34 W/m² is a recently-detected trend, not a static feature. The dome's geometry is inherently asymmetric (NP at center, sun at varying radii), but the model provides no radiation budget derivation connecting dome geometry to any specific W/m² value — the observation is adopted wholesale without prediction. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
tldr_evidence: Earth's hemispheric energy balance is remarkably symmetric — a key finding by Stephens et al. (2012). The small +0.34 W/m² northern excess is a recently-detected trend driven by the northern hemisphere having more land and different cloud patterns. The dome's geometry (concentrated energy near the center, less at the rim) can't produce a hemisphere-dependent asymmetry.
tldr_verdict: The hemispheric energy balance is nearly symmetric, with the small excess explained by land/ocean distribution. The dome's axially symmetric geometry can't produce hemisphere-dependent effects.

finding: Dome's own H(r) implies ≥38% gravity drop at the Antarctic rim under any monotonic g↔H(r) coupling: 1,430× apples-to-apples error under linear coupling, ~590× under the most-forgiving H^(1/5) scaling, with 170× as a dimensionless lower bound from the whole-globe pole-equator spread.
detail_claim: Low gravity anomaly in Antarctica correlates with dome's 'Sub-terrestrial structure' (Domain 19 of live-power page).
detail_evidence:
The dome model's height function H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km gives: at the pole (r=0): H = 8,537 km. At the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 20,015 km): H = 8,537 × exp(−20,015/8,619) = 8,537 × 0.098 = 837 km. The dome's stated narrative ties gravity to aetheric density variation — Domain 4 names the mechanism “Dielectric pressure with aetheric density variation” (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 69) — and ties aetheric pressure to disc geometry: “The disc edge creates an aetheric pressure gradient. That gradient drives both the anomalous geomagnetic field minimum (SAA) and the anomalous atmospheric pressure pattern (Roaring 40s)” (raw-text/02-wins.txt line 31). The dome does not, as of V51.2, publish a sentence that explicitly says g = f(H(r)). It does, however, choose its only quantitative gravity formula's exponential decay length λg = 8,619 km to be identical to the H(r) decay constant 8,619 km (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 70 vs. raw-text/05-model.txt line 16) — a length-scale identity that is otherwise unexplained. The argument that follows therefore proceeds as a conditional rather than an attribution: IF the dome's narrative is taken at face value — aetheric pressure depends on column height H(r), gravity depends on aetheric density, and the shared 8,619 km length scale is meaningful — THEN gravitational effects must depend monotonically on H(r), and the 10× difference in H(r) between pole and rim must translate to substantially different gravity. The only escape is to deny the conditional — to say the dome's only quantitative gravity formula (Domain 4, treated below) is causally independent of H(r) despite sharing its decay constant — in which case the dome has no dome-specific gravity mechanism at all, only a WGS84-clone reskin (the second self-contradiction documented after the H(r) chain). Under linear scaling: g_rim/g_pole = 837/8,537 = 0.098, a 90% drop. Under square-root scaling: √(837/8,537) ≈ 0.31, a 69% drop. These two examples bracket the range of plausible aetheric-pressure scalings; even an extreme g ∝ H1/5 coupling still gives a 38% drop — two orders of magnitude larger than the observed 0.53%. To reconcile any such coupling with the observed 0.53% variation, the function relating H(r) to gravity would need to be essentially flat over a 10× variation in H(r) — at which point H(r) is irrelevant to gravity and the stated mechanism is vacuous. If the dome instead claims gravity is independent of H(r), it must specify an alternative mechanism consistent with the observed 0.53% variation; no such mechanism appears in V51.0. What is actually observed? Gravity varies by only 0.53% from equator to pole (9.780 to 9.832 m/s²), as measured by Swarm and GRACE satellites orbiting at 250–500 km altitude — well below the dome's alleged firmament of ~9,086 km, meaning they measure gravitational acceleration within the dome's own predicted field. The dome predicts ~90% gravity variation (factor of ~10 in g between pole and rim); reality shows 0.53% variation (factor of 1.005). The apples-to-apples comparison (both in m/s²): the dome predicts an 8.87 m/s² gravity drop from polar gravity to Antarctic-rim gravity; the observed Antarctic gravity drop from the polar reference is only 0.0062 m/s² — a 1,430× error. As a conservative dimensionless lower bound: the dome's predicted fractional variation (90%) vs. observed global fractional variation (0.53%) gives 90%/0.53% ≈ 170× — a ratio of dimensionless percentages computed on different baselines, which understates the true discrepancy. Either way, the formula that derives from dome geometry fails by 170–1,430× depending on whether the comparison is apples-to-apples (absolute m/s²) or dimensionless-ratio (fractional variation). This is not a matter of calibration or measurement uncertainty; it is a qualitative failure of the dome's gravity mechanism. The Antarctic gravity low has multiple well-characterized standard-physics causes: (1) crustal thickness variations and a sub-lithospheric mantle plume beneath the East Antarctic Plateau producing a broad negative anomaly — a geophysically localized feature strongest in the Ross Sea/Marie Byrd Land region, not the azimuthally symmetric pattern the dome's radial H(r) would predict (Pappa et al., 2019, JGR); (2) incomplete post-glacial isostatic adjustment from the Last Glacial Maximum ice sheet, measured directly by GRACE satellite gravimetry (Shepherd et al., 2012, Science); and (3) dynamic topography from mantle convection. These mechanisms are independently constrained by seismic imaging, explain both the magnitude and the azimuthal asymmetry of the anomaly, and do not require invoking undefined mechanisms. See Sections 2.3 and 2.8.

A deeper problem emerges from the dome repository's own code. The gravity formula used in monitor.py (Domain 4 gravity-anomaly entry) is g(r) = 9.7803 × (1 + 0.005307 × exp(−r/8619)). Every coefficient is WGS84-derived: 9.7803 m/s² is the WGS84 normal gravity at the equator (match: 0.003%), 0.005307 is the WGS84 gravity flattening coefficient (match: 0.09%), and the formula evaluates to 9.8322 m/s² — the WGS84 normal gravity at the pole (match: 0.0002%). The monitor code evaluates at r = 0 only, where exp(0/8619) = 1, collapsing the dome-specific exponential entirely: g = 9.7803 × (1 + 0.005307 × 1) = 9.8322. This is a tautological identity between WGS84 constants — the dome’s own code admits as much, with the source field on that line reading “WGS84 standard” (V51.2 live-power page, Domain 4 equatorial gravity entry; identical formula in V51.1 monitor.py). The dome has two incompatible gravity stories: (1) the H(r) column-height mechanism, which predicts a 90% gravity drop at the Antarctic rim and fails catastrophically; and (2) this monitor.py formula, which predicts only 0.48% variation and matches reality — but only because it is the globe’s answer expressed in dome notation. At the only other geometrically-motivated evaluation point r = 20,015 km, Domain 4’s formula predicts g ≈ 9.7854 m/s², which violates Domain 4’s own published falsification criterion (“Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard”, raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 71) by 4.8×. The formula that works is wholly WGS84; the formula that derives from dome geometry fails by 170–1,430× depending on the comparison — 170× is the dimensionless-fractional lower bound (90% predicted variation vs 0.53% observed) computed on different baselines and deliberately understates the discrepancy, while 1,430× is the apples-to-apples absolute-m/s² miss at Antarctic latitude (see the opening paragraph).

The Mascon ↔ SAA r=0.92 correlation is not a dome-specific prediction. A dome defender might press Domain 19 (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 152) — “Mascon Gravity ↔ SAA Boundary: r=0.92” labelled ‘Sub-terrestrial structure’ — as a genuinely high empirical regularity between two global anomaly fields, and ask whether we engage it on its own terms. The r=0.92 is real, but it indicates shared causation, not independent prediction. Standard geophysics supplies the shared cause: East Antarctic crustal roots plus the uncompensated sub-lithospheric mantle plume (Pappa et al., 2019, JGR) produce both the crustal gravity low via isostatic imbalance and, through the associated lithospheric/mantle structure, contribute to the geometry of the SAA field minimum. The co-location of the two anomaly fields is a downstream consequence of common-cause geology, not a novel forecast. The dome’s contribution is to relabel the shared cause “Sub-terrestrial structure” without supplying any equation mapping dome geometry (disc radius, H(r), aetheric parameters) to either field — so r=0.92 is empirical regularity, not forecast.

The refraction cascade (Section 1.8) adds a further constraint: at the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 15,500–20,015 km), the firmament ceiling H(r) ≈ 1,400–840 km.
detail_verdict_text: The dome's own H(r) predicts an 8.87 m/s² gravity drop from polar to Antarctic-rim gravity; the observed drop is 0.0062 m/s² — a 1,430× error (conservative dimensionless bound: 90%/0.53% ≈ 170×, comparing fractional percentages on different baselines). The dome invokes the undefined label 'Sub-terrestrial structure' (V51.2, Domain 19 of live-power page) with no published mechanism, equations, or physical content. The Antarctic gravity low is primarily explained by an isostatically uncompensated mantle plume beneath the East Antarctic Plateau, with contributions from post-glacial isostatic adjustment. Standard geophysics explains the feature without invoking undefined 'sump nodes.' Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from GRACE/GRACE-FO Level-2 gravity products (JPL archive, continuous coverage since 2002) and published Bouguer-anomaly compilations (Pappa et al. 2019, JGR, DOI 10.1029/2019JB017997; Ferraccioli et al. 2011, Nature; Forte 2010) — all publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
detail_extra:
Closing three lines of dome defense (anticipated). A dome defender facing our original critique has three lines of retort — on terminology, citation numbering, and the 170× ratio. Each folds under the dome’s own live-power page.

(i) “I never said ‘toroidal sump node’ — show me the sentence.” Earlier dome snapshots labelled the Antarctic correlation with phrasing we have been unable to locate in current V51.2 raw text. The V51.2 live-power page now prints a different label: “Mascon Gravity ↔ SAA Boundary: r=0.92 (Sub-terrestrial structure)” (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 152). The attack does not depend on any particular label. Whatever the dome chooses to call the feature, no published equation links dome geometry to the observed anomaly, the correlation line has no mechanism field, and the label — whether ‘sump node’ or ‘Sub-terrestrial structure’ — is a rebrand of a phenomenon that standard geophysics (mantle plume beneath East Antarctica + post-glacial isostatic adjustment, measured continuously by GRACE / GRACE-FO since 2002) has explained since Ferraccioli et al. 2011. The critique of V51.0 terminology carries over verbatim to V51.2: an undefined label is not a mechanism.

(ii) “Your 170× ratio is headline-inflated — you compared the whole-globe pole-equator spread, but the disc’s ‘rim’ maps to the geographic Antarctic, so compare against Antarctic-latitude gravity.” A dome defender’s objection is well-founded that the apples-to-apples comparison is the dome’s prediction at r = 20,015 km versus the real gravity measured at the geographic location that radius maps to (the South Pole and interior Antarctica) — and that objection concedes in the same breath that the ratio there is “larger still.” It is. Anchoring to the WGS84 polar value 9.8322 m/s², the dome’s linear H(r)→g coupling at r = 20,015 km predicts g ≈ 9.8322 × (837/8,537) = 0.964 m/s² — an 8.868 m/s² reduction from the polar reference. The observed Antarctic-latitude gravity at the value cited in the objection — ~9.826 m/s², which the WGS84 Somigliana formula places at ~70°S (Antarctic coast / Southern Ocean transition; true interior 80–90°S sits at 9.8306–9.8322 m/s², so this anchoring is geographically conservative and the ratio rises to ~5,600× if interior gravity is used instead) — is 0.0062 m/s² below the polar reference, a 0.063% reduction. Dividing predicted drop by observed drop gives ≈ 1,430×, almost an order of magnitude worse than the 170× headline (which uses the whole-globe pole-equator spread as the denominator; the 1,430× figure is the rigorous apples-to-apples comparison at Antarctic latitude and strengthens rather than replaces the 170× figure). Recomputing against the actual Mascon-Bouguer anomaly (-40 to -60 mGal ≈ 5×10-4 m/s², per Ferraccioli et al. 2011) widens the miss by another order of magnitude (to ~17,700×). The honest correction does not shrink the critique; it deepens it.

(iii) “Domain 12 is Polaris Excess, not gravity — your citation was misdirected.” Conceded and corrected: the parenthetical in the WGS84-tautology paragraph above now reads “Domain 4 gravity-anomaly entry,” matching V51.2 live-power numbering (live.html, raw-text/08-live-power.txt lines 67–70). The correction anchors rather than loosens the argument. Domain 4 states verbatim: Formula: g(r) = 9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(−r/λg)); Falsification: Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard (line 71). The dome is therefore explicitly calibrating its only quantitative gravity formula against the globe model — the falsification criterion does not merely permit the globe answer, it requires it. The 0.5% variation the additive form allows is not a dome-specific prediction; it is produced by k = 0.005307 — the WGS84 gravity flattening coefficient — multiplying an exponential that evaluates to 1 at r=0 (where monitor.py anchors it, reproducing the WGS84 polar value by construction) and decays to exp(−1) ≈ 0.37 at r=8,619 km. At r=20,015 km — the Antarctic rim, the only other geometrically-motivated evaluation point — Domain 4 predicts g ≈ 9.7854 m/s², which violates Domain 4’s own “Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard” criterion by 4.8×. Any reader who says “but the dome’s formula is additive with a small k, not the multiplicative scalings you attacked” is pointing at the smoking gun: the small-effect formulation exists only because it is the globe’s formula expressed in dome notation. Domain 19 (“Mascon Gravity,” line 127–128) carries only a one-sided threshold: Anomaly <20 mGal. The real East Antarctic Bouguer anomaly is roughly -40 to -60 mGal, so any honest measurement trivially exceeds the threshold and counts as a “PASS” on the dome’s scoreboard by construction. That is pass-by-threshold, not pass-by-prediction — the falsification logic is inverted.

Net effect. The three-point defense either concedes the underlying critique on every leg (the label change, the corrected parenthetical, the deeper Antarctic-latitude comparison) or requires the dome author to publicly strike the Domain 4 falsification text (Model error >0.1% vs WGS84 standard), strike the Domain 19 one-sided threshold (Anomaly <20 mGal), and publish a mechanism equation linking “Sub-terrestrial structure” to a specific value of g(r = 20,015). None of those edits has been made. The author’s own methodology statement If prediction falls outside tolerance, it fails — no exceptions (live-power “HOW THIS WORKS”) binds him to the standard he has just shown cannot be met for this WIN. A defender may retreat by reframing Domains 4 and 19 as limited-scope reference checks rather than model-discriminating tests; that retreat concedes the substantive point — neither closed-form formula constitutes a dome-specific forecast — and leaves the fundamental question (what equation, in dome geometry, predicts the observed 9.832/9.780 m/s² gradient without globe parameters?) unanswered.
tldr_evidence: The dome's own height function H(r) collapses by a factor of ten between the pole and the Antarctic rim, so any monotonic coupling of gravity to column height predicts a large gravity drop there — 90% under linear coupling, ~38% even under the most-forgiving H^(1/5) scaling. At ~70°S (the Antarctic coast / Southern Ocean transition latitude where the WGS84 Somigliana formula gives 9.826 m/s²) — a deliberately conservative anchor against the dome defender's own choice — measured gravity is just 0.0062 m/s² (0.063%) below the polar reference. Dividing the dome's predicted 8.87 m/s² drop (linear coupling) by this 0.006 m/s² measured drop gives 1,430×; ~590× under H^(1/5); 170× from the dimensionless whole-globe spread; ~5,600× if anchored to true interior (80°S, 9.8306 m/s²). The dome invokes an undefined 'Sub-terrestrial structure' correlation (Domain 19 of live-power page) with no published mechanism or equations.
tldr_verdict: The dome's own height formula predicts 90% gravity loss at the Antarctic rim under linear g↔H(r) coupling (38% even under the gentlest H^(1/5) scaling). Comparing to the actual Antarctic-latitude measurement gives a 1,430× error under linear coupling; ~590× under H^(1/5); 170× as a conservative dimensionless lower bound from the whole-globe spread. The 'Sub-terrestrial structure' escape hatch has no equations and no measurements.

finding: Non-discriminating: Chapman (1933) ionospheric mechanism + Kim & Chang (2018) predict the 39-eclipse ensemble signal magnitude and geometry. The dome publishes a scaling equation (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) relevant to its 2026 forward projection (WIN-010), but the 1991–2016 Kim & Chang ensemble evaluated here attaches no dome-derived numerical prediction — it is a retrospective citation of observatory data. Duplicates WIN-010 and WIN-025 observational basis at a larger aggregation level.
detail_claim: Ensemble analysis of 207 geomagnetic manifestations from 100 INTERMAGNET observatories across 39 solar eclipses (1991–2016) shows statistically significant magnetic field changes during eclipses. The dome model claims this confirms 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' via the pressurized toroidal cavity's induction gradient.
detail_evidence:
The underlying data is real and well-documented. Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) conducted a rigorous statistical analysis of geomagnetic field variations during 39 solar eclipses using INTERMAGNET data. They confirmed systematic patterns: Y-component increases, X/Z/F decreases, strongest at magnetic latitudes 30°–50°, over ~180-minute windows centered on maximum eclipse. This is genuine, peer-reviewed science.

For a separate internal-consistency concern applicable to the dome's own mechanism (not the Std Model Explains verdict above): the magnetic signal tracks apparent rather than physical shadow geometry, so Chapman's ionospheric mechanism is unaffected by the dome's refraction indeterminacy. But the dome's own claimed cavity mechanism does invoke physical geometry, and the refraction fix leaves physical sun altitude indeterminate — undermining the geometric shadow it claims for all 39 eclipses. See Section 1.8.

The mechanism has been well understood since Chapman (1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3). During a solar eclipse, the Moon's shadow reduces photoionization in the E-region ionosphere (90–150 km altitude), decreasing electron density and electrical conductivity. This weakens the solar-quiet (Sq) dynamo current system. The Sq conductivity framework (Yamazaki & Maute 2021) predicts the qualitative pattern — Y-component increase, X/Z/F decrease, peak at eclipse maximum, strongest at Sq-vortex latitudes (~30°–50°) — from measured ionospheric electron content depletion. The dome provides no derivation showing how its cavity geometry produces the specific component patterns, the latitude dependence, or the 180-minute timescale. The standard model derives all three from the geometry of the Sq current vortex. Crucially, the ionospheric parameters Sq models use (E-region electron density, conductivity tensors) are obtained from independent sources — ionosonde networks, GPS/TEC, and climatological averages — none of which are fitted to the eclipse magnetic signal being predicted. The 39-eclipse ensemble evaluated here does not invoke FSF at all — it is a retrospective citation of the Kim & Chang (2018) 1991–2016 dataset with no dome-derived prediction attached. For completeness: the dome's FSF scaling factor (relevant to the WIN-010 2026 forward projection) is a legitimate geometric derivation — direct recomputation reproduces the nine published FSF values at Pearson r = 0.9999, so FSF = H(r)/r is real and we retract our prior "empirical fitting" framing. That concession is germane to WIN-010, not WIN-068. For WIN-068, FSF is a spectator: the ensemble statistical method derives its expected signal amplitude from the 39-eclipse dataset itself, not from dome geometry.

The dome's Steelman Evidence Ladder places eclipse geomagnetic evidence at Tier 1, explicitly acknowledging it is consistent with both models — an admission that WIN-068 alone cannot distinguish dome cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics. This WIN also duplicates the observational basis of WIN-010 (BOU 2017 eclipse, −10.9 nT) and WIN-025 (2024 9-station confirmation). All three cite the same physical phenomenon at different aggregation levels: single-station, 9-station, and 39-eclipse ensemble. The dome counts each as a separate confirmed prediction, inflating the WIN total to three entries for what remains a single non-discriminating mechanism — three observational confirmations of Chapman Sq, not three independent dome-derived predictions.
detail_verdict_text:
Non-discriminating: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism — first described by Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3) and quantitatively confirmed across 39 eclipses by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) using INTERMAGNET data — predicts the ensemble signal (Y-component increase, X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30°–50° over ~180-minute windows) without any dome parameters. The moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, suppressing E-region conductivity and the Sq current system; the resulting magnetic perturbation tracks the eclipse shadow path with predictable amplitude and timing. This mechanism operates across all 39 eclipses in the dome's ensemble and does not require the dome's cavity geometry, firmament height, or any other disc parameter.

Credit where it is due on the scaling itself: the dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses a field-strength factor that genuinely equals H(r)/r normalized to Boulder. Solving the dome's own V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) for each station's latitude and computing (H(r)/r)/(H(rBOU)/rBOU) reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst (Pearson r = 0.9999 between predicted and claimed). Our prior framing of FSF as "empirical fitting, not dome-geometric derivation" was quantitatively wrong and has been retracted in the WIN-010/025/068 cluster; the dome's FSF really is dome geometry.

Being right about the scaling law between stations is not the same as predicting the signal. The 39-eclipse ensemble counted here is a retrospective citation of Kim & Chang (2018): FSF does not enter the 1991–2016 data at all — it is only invoked for the 2026 forward projection. For that projection, the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor (−10.9 nT) is adopted from the 2017 observation, not derived from H(r), n(r), or any other dome parameter; every other station's predicted delta_Z is the 2017 BOU magnitude scaled by the station's obscuration coverage and its FSF = H(r)/r geometric ratio. Standard Sq modeling, by contrast, derives absolute magnitudes at each station from parameters sourced independently — E-region electron density from ionosonde networks, GPS/TEC, and climatological averages — none of which are fitted to the eclipse signal being predicted. The dome reuses the specific 2017 BOU observation as both a counted WIN (WIN-010) and the 2026 baseline, which is in-sample evaluation, not out-of-sample calibration. The scaling shape is also not uniquely dome-geometric: any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with r·tan(θ) = H(r) produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form, so matching one parametrization to 0.1% does not discriminate the dome from any alternative disc-geometry model with a comparable height function.

The dome's own Steelman Evidence Ladder places eclipse geomagnetic evidence at Tier 1, explicitly acknowledging it is consistent with both models — an admission that WIN-068 alone cannot distinguish dome cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics.

Even granting that FSF = H(r)/r is a valid geometric scaling, Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse) shows the disc-radius values that scaling feeds on are themselves computed from observed Polaris elevation angles, travelling through the same n(r) medium the dome concedes is 300–6,000× more refractive than Earth's atmosphere. Every dome-coordinate r in the FSF table inherits that contamination, so even the one genuine derivation the dome has produced anchors on numbers that are not what the dome thinks they are.

WIN-010 (BOU 2017), WIN-025 (2024 9-station), and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble 1991–2016) all cite observations explained by the same ionospheric Sq mechanism at three different aggregation levels. The dome counts each as a separate confirmed prediction, inflating the score count without adding independent evidence.

Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails the post-hoc test. The confirming data comes from Kim & Chang's published dataset covering 1991–2016, publicly available years before the dome incorporated it (Kim & Chang published in 2018; the dome incorporated it only in V51.0, early 2026 — an eight-year gap). Under the dome author's own timestamping definition, no pre-registration of the expected value in a venue independent of the author could be located. The monitor script has no validation logic for WIN-068 (monitoring = "none"), and the 39-eclipse ensemble is a retrospective literature citation, not a live prediction.
tldr_evidence: Eclipse geomagnetic perturbations have been understood since Chapman (1933) — the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric conductivity and suppresses Sq currents. The dome's ensemble of 39 eclipses spanning 1991–2016 (Kim & Chang 2018) confirms this ionospheric mechanism; it does not require or validate dome cavity dynamics.
tldr_verdict: The Chapman mechanism (documented since 1933 and confirmed across 39 eclipses by Kim & Chang 2018) predicts the ensemble signal without any dome parameters. The dome does genuinely derive the station-to-station FSF scaling (FSF = H(r)/r, Pearson r = 0.9999) — we retract our earlier empirical-fitting framing — but the 39-eclipse ensemble counted here is a retrospective Kim & Chang citation that never invokes FSF, and the 2026 forward projection still imports its absolute magnitude from the 2017 Boulder observation. Triple-counting WIN-010/025/068 for one mechanism inflates the score.

Eclipse magnetic effects track apparent shadow geometry (Chapman Sq), not physical sun position. Curmudgeon gate 2 analysis confirmed.

finding: 6.2% RMSE is orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy; same-hemisphere routes (e.g. Australia to UK) also diverge at 6.2%, which V13 predicts should be its best case. AGD66→GDA94 shift is routine datum improvement, not evidence of topology
detail_claim: The V13 two-zone topology with aetheric refraction predicts that local straight southern controls remain exact while long transcontinental Australian routes expand beyond globe great-circle predictions. 12 roads and 9 cross-links yield 6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE. The AGD66→GDA94 datum shift is claimed as evidence of non-geocentric topology.
detail_evidence:
The kernel of truth: Australian road distances do exceed great-circle (geodesic) distances. This is expected — roads go around mountains, follow valleys, and deviate for terrain. The Nullarbor Plain's road-to-geodesic ratio approaches 1.00 on its dead-straight central segment (~146 km), though the full Norseman–Ceduna crossing computes closer to ~1.07. The 90-Mile Straight itself (146.6 km, Balladonia to Caiguna) deviates from its great-circle geodesic by less than 0.4% — effectively zero for a physically straight road on the ellipsoid, and consistent with the ~1.00 ratio above. Either way, the near-zero excess reflects flat terrain, not dome topology — it's a geographic fact, not a dome prediction. The systematic excess on longer routes is a well-known property of road networks: longer routes accumulate more deviations from the geodesic. The dome model claims its V13 topology 'predicts' this excess, but any model that adds positive perturbations to distances will match the pattern.

The 6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE and 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error are presented as validation, but by geodetic standards they are catastrophic. Modern geodesy (using GDA2020, itself built on the geocentric WGS84/ITRF framework) achieves millimeter-level accuracy in positioning and sub-meter accuracy in distance. The dome model's best evidence — its Tier 1 in the 'Steelman Evidence Ladder' — has errors roughly five orders of magnitude larger than standard sub-meter geodetic positioning (~250,000×: a 6.2% residual on a ~4,000 km transcontinental baseline amounts to ~250 km of positional error, while routine GNSS positioning achieves sub-meter absolute accuracy on the same baseline). The gap widens to roughly eight orders of magnitude against mm-level GNSS and nine orders against sub-mm VLBI geodesy. If this is the strongest evidence, it reveals how weak the evidence base is.

The AGD66→GDA94 argument fundamentally misunderstands geodetic datum evolution. AGD66 was non-geocentric by design: it used the Australian National Spheroid fitted to local control points at Johnston Origin, with its center offset from the Earth's center of mass. This was standard practice for regional datums worldwide in the 1960s (NAD27 in North America, ED50 in Europe — all non-geocentric). The ~200 m shift to GDA94 was not a 'correction' revealing hidden topology — it was the predictable result of transitioning from a regionally-optimized datum to a globally-consistent geocentric one, enabled by satellite positioning technology (GPS/VLBI/SLR) that did not exist in 1966. Every continent underwent the same type of shift. The 'northeast' direction of the shift in Australia reflects the specific offset of the ANS ellipsoid from the geocenter, not some property of dome geometry.

The dome claims 'the globe needs datum resets and vertical patches where the ovoid cavity geometry predicts the expansion directly.' This inverts reality: datum improvements are refinements that improve accuracy from meters to millimeters. The dome model, by contrast, achieves 6.2% errors — that's errors measured in hundreds of kilometers on transcontinental routes. The globe framework doesn't need the dome's 'prediction'; it achieves accuracy the dome cannot approach.

Finally, the V13 prediction uses parameters calibrated against globe-framework distances. The disc radius a = 20,015 km = π × R_Earth, and the refraction profile n(r) contains H(r) derived from globe-calibrated measurements. The 'prediction' is circular: dome parameters are set to approximately reproduce globe distances, then the approximate reproduction is claimed as confirmation.

The strongest dome defense is that standard geodesy also cannot directly predict road distances — Vincenty's formula gives geodesic distances, not road routes. This is true, but terrain-based routing models (using digital elevation and road network data) explain road excess as a function of topographic roughness — the detour-index / road-circuity literature (Huber & Rust 2016, R Journal; Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Environment and Planning B: Planning and Design) documents typical road-network detour indices of ~1.05–1.20 as a terrain-driven function, producing sub-1% mean-ratio prediction errors against observed road distances with a single free parameter (V13's same-hemisphere mean error on this same metric is 10.2%, with ≥4 free parameters). The Nullarbor Plain's near-zero road excess occurs precisely where the terrain is flat. If the dome's V13 topology were responsible for the excess, flat terrain should still produce it — topology curves space regardless of terrain. The geographic fact that road excess correlates with terrain roughness, not latitude or topology, is itself evidence that terrain, not topology, drives the pattern. The dome chose road distances as its validation metric; on that metric, standard topographic analysis outperforms dome topology by orders of magnitude.

One additional concern: the dome cites 12 roads and 9 cross-links as its validation sample from thousands of possible Australian road pairs. No selection criteria are disclosed. With routes spanning different terrains, latitudes, and distances, a non-representative sample could easily bias the apparent accuracy figure in either direction. The dome has not shown that the selected routes were chosen before analysis or that unfavorable routes were not excluded — the hallmarks of cherry-picking.
detail_verdict_text: The 6.2% RMSE is roughly 6× worse than typical sub-meter GPS positioning on 4,000 km baselines, and orders of magnitude worse than millimeter-precision geodetic standards — making this the dome's strongest evidence also its most damning comparison. Road distances exceed geodesics for geographic reasons (terrain), not topology. The AGD66→GDA94 datum shift is a routine improvement from regional to global reference frames, identical to transitions on every continent. The dome's V13 parameters are calibrated against globe distances (a = π×R_Earth), making the 'validation' circular. Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The numerical value is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters (disc radius, firmament height, chosen symmetry axis, or similar), and the "confirmation" consists of matching that derivation to a globe-measured value — which, because the dome parameters were themselves fit to globe data (see Kill-Shot #1 and Part 2b), is circular. There is no pre-registration of the derivation in a venue that timestamps independently of the author, and the dome monitor script (where one exists) encodes the expected value in the same commit as the comparison. Under the dome author's own definition and our stricter standard in Part 3.1b, this is retrodiction, not prediction.

code_analysis.derives_from_dome_evidence: V13 topology + refractive profile are dome-derived; numerical parameters are fit to globe data (AGD66→GDA94), but the geometric framework (ring/toroidal, V13) originates from dome model structure.

tldr_evidence: Australian road distances do exceed great-circle distances — but that's because roads go around mountains and follow terrain. The dome's 6.2% RMSE on road distances is ~250,000× worse than standard sub-meter geodetic accuracy — about five orders of magnitude. The Nullarbor Plain straight road deviates only 0.4% from the geodesic, suggesting the 6.2% figure mostly measures road curviness, not dome geometry.
tldr_verdict: The 6.2% RMSE is ~250,000× worse than standard sub-meter geodesy — five orders of magnitude. Most of the error measures road curviness (terrain, detours), not flat-disc geometry — the straightest road segment confirms this with only 0.4% deviation.
